courseTitle: Tracing Blueprints for Site Plans — Municipality Focus
courseDescription: Practical, beginner-focused course on tracing and preparing site plans from blueprints for municipal submission (regular and irregular plots), with special emphasis on municipality rules: setbacks, right-of-way, contours, and site annotations.
courseObjectives: Trace and digitize paper or scanned blueprints accurately
Handle regular and irregular plot boundaries
Apply municipal setback, right-of-way (ROW), and building line rules
Create a complete site plan ready for municipality submission

phases.phaseTitle: Prepare the Drawing — Scale, Units, and Reference
phases.phaseDescription: Set up your drawing correctly before tracing to avoid scale errors.

steps.title: Import or Scan the Blueprint
steps.subtitle: Attach the scanned image as an underlay
steps.why: A clean underlay lets you trace accurately without altering the original file.
instructions: Scan the blueprint at high resolution (300 dpi or higher). Save as PDF or PNG.
In AutoCAD type ATTACH (or IMAGE) and select the file to insert as an underlay.
Place the underlay near origin and lock its layer to avoid accidental moves.

note.label: Info
note.text: Lock the underlay layer and set it to a faded color for visibility.

steps.title: Set Units and Scale — Match the Blueprint
steps.subtitle: Ensure drawing units match the blueprint units (meters/mm)
steps.why: Tracing at the wrong units causes incorrect dimensions and rework during municipal checks.
instructions: Open UNITS and set units to Millimeters (or Meters) depending on municipality format.
If the underlay is not to scale: find a known dimension on the blueprint (for example, site frontage) and use SCALE to match it.
Use the DIST command to verify a known distance on the image and compare it to the printed dimension.

practicalExample.scenario: Site has a 20 m road frontage
practicalExample.action: Measure on the image, compute scale factor, use SCALE with reference points.

steps.title: Set Layers and Drawing Standards
steps.subtitle: Create standard layers for boundaries, setbacks, contours, buildings, text
steps.why: Organized layers make verification and printing straightforward for municipal submission.
instructions: Create layers: SITE_BOUNDARY, SETBACK, ROW, CONTOUR, BUILDING, TEXT, DIMENSION, TITLEBLOCK, UNDERLAY
Set standard colors and lineweights (e.g., SITE_BOUNDARY = red, BUILDING = cyan, SETBACK = magenta dashed).
Keep the underlay on the UNDERLAY layer and lock it.

phases.phaseTitle: Trace Site Boundary — Regular and Irregular Plots
phases.phaseDescription: Techniques to trace clean, accurate site boundaries for both regular (rectangular) and irregular plots.

steps.title: Trace Regular Plots — Use Rectangle and Polyline
steps.subtitle: Fast method for square/rectangular sites
steps.why: Regular plots are common and can be drawn precisely using simple tools.
instructions: Use RECTANGLE for perfect rectangles: snap corners to underlay reference points.
If dimensions are given, draw to exact length using the command line entry.
Convert rectangle to polyline (if needed) to apply lineweights and hatch.

steps.title: Trace Irregular Plots — Polyline by Coordinates
steps.subtitle: Trace each vertex carefully and close the polyline
steps.why: Irregular boundaries require precise vertex placement to compute areas and setbacks correctly.
instructions: Enable Object Snaps (OSNAP) to Endpoint, Intersection, Midpoint for accuracy.
Trace each boundary segment with PLINE, snapping to the underlay image at each corner.
Close the polyline with C or use JOIN if segments are separate.
Use AREA to verify site area matches blueprint label (if provided).

note.label: Warning
note.text: If the underlay is skewed, first use ALIGN with three reference points to correct rotation.

steps.title: Clean Up Boundary — Simplify and Snap
steps.subtitle: Remove overlapping segments and ensure topology is correct
steps.why: Municipal checks fail when boundaries have gaps, overlaps, or non-closed polylines.
instructions: Use OVERKILL to remove duplicate segments and tiny slivers.
Use PEDIT > Join to merge segments into single polylines.
Zoom and inspect every vertex for small gaps; use FILLET 0 to snap coincident endpoints if necessary.

phases.phaseTitle: Apply Municipal Rules — Setbacks, ROW, and Building Lines
phases.phaseDescription: Implement municipality-specific setbacks, road setbacks, and other regulatory constraints.

steps.title: Understand Municipal Setback Classes
steps.subtitle: Residential vs commercial vs mixed-use setbacks
steps.why: Different land use categories enforce different building line distances; apply correct class to avoid rejection.
instructions: Check  municipality document for setback rules (front, rear, side). Typical example for pokhara municipality: Front setback = 2m (residential), Side = 1.5m, Rear = 2m — verify exact current values.
Record the correct values in the drawing notes in the TITLEBLOCK or a dedicated NOTES layer.

note.label: Info
note.text: If official rules change, update your template header and notify the team.

steps.title: Handle Right-Of-Way (ROW) and Road Setbacks
steps.subtitle: Apply ROW widths and future road widening zones
steps.why: Municipal ROW influences buildable area and parking requirements.
instructions: If the site borders a road, trace the road centerline and offset half ROW on both sides to show full ROW.
Mark future widening by offsetting additional zone in a dashed style on ROW layer.
Note the road classification (local, arterial) in drawing notes — Municipal rules may vary by road class.

steps.title: Create Building Lines and Buildable Area
steps.subtitle: Offset setbacks from the site boundary
steps.why: Building lines show where construction is allowed and are critical for municipal approval.
instructions: Use OFFSET to create setback lines from the SITE_BOUNDARY polyline: front, rear, and sides as per municipal rules.
Label each setback line with its class (e.g., FRONT_SETBACK) on the SETBACK layer.
The area inside the setback lines is the buildable area; consider hatching it lightly for visualization.

steps.title: Create Setback Offsets — OFFSET and POLYOFFSET
steps.subtitle: Generate setback lines from the site boundary
steps.why: Setback lines visually show where building footprints are allowed.
instructions: Select the SITE_BOUNDARY polyline and use OFFSET with the setback distance (e.g., OFFSET 3000 for 3m).
Place the offset to the inside for building setback (or outside for buffer/ROW visualization).
Label each offset line on the SETBACK layer with its class name (FRONT_SETBACK, SIDE_SETBACK).

phases.phaseTitle: Add Site Details — Contours, Existing Structures, Utilities
phases.phaseDescription: Trace contours and locate existing structures/utilities for a complete site plan.

steps.title: Trace Contours and Spot Levels
steps.subtitle: Digitize elevation lines and labels
steps.why: Contours and spot levels help assess grading, cut/fill, and drainage for site design.
instructions: Trace contour lines on the CONTOUR layer using PLINE; keep lineweights light.
Add text labels for contour elevations on TEXT layer; maintain consistent text height.
If contours are dense or unclear, simplify using smooth polylines but keep critical elevation points accurate.

steps.title: Locate Existing Buildings, Trees, and Utilities
steps.subtitle: Mark existing conditions clearly
steps.why: Municipal reviewers need existing features shown for approval and impact assessment.
instructions: Trace building footprints on BUILDING layer and mark entrances, eaves, and chimneys if relevant.
Mark utilities (water, sewer, electric) with standard symbols and note their locations.
Use hatch patterns to show paved areas, lawns, or impervious surfaces on HATCH layer.

phases.phaseTitle: Cutting, Filling and Grading — Calculate Basic Earthworks
phases.phaseDescription: Beginner-friendly steps to estimate cut/fill and show grading sketches for municipal submission.

steps.title: Simple Cut/Fill Zones — Use Spot Levels and Sections
steps.subtitle: Mark cut and fill areas with approximate depths
steps.why: Municipal review needs an idea of grading impact and drainage paths.
instructions: Place spot levels at key points and calculate difference from proposed finished level to existing level.
Sketch simple cross-sections for steep sites showing cut/fill extent and retaining need.
Label cut as negative values and fill as positive values on the drawing.

steps.title: Define Drainage Paths and Surface Flow
steps.subtitle: Show direction arrows and low points
steps.why: Proper drainage prevents water pooling and reduces erosion — reviewers check this carefully.
instructions: Add flow arrows from higher contours to lower areas on the drawing.
Show proposed drainage features: swales, storm drain inlets, and direction of flow.
Ensure building finished floor levels (FFL) are above adjacent drainage low points.

phases.phaseTitle: Finalize Drawing — Annotations, Dimensions, and Checklist
phases.phaseDescription: Prepare the final site plan with required annotations and a municipal-ready checklist.

steps.title: Add Dimensions and North Arrow
steps.subtitle: Clear dimensions and orientation are mandatory
steps.why: Reviewers must be able to verify distances, setbacks, and orientation at a glance.
instructions: Dimension overall site extents and building setbacks clearly on DIMENSION layer.
Place a north arrow symbol in the title block area or near the site on a dedicated layer.
Include scale text and a scale bar if needed.

steps.title: Title Block, Notes, and Submission Checklist
steps.subtitle: Complete metadata required by municipality
steps.why: Incomplete title blocks or missing notes lead to rejection or delay.
instructions: Fill title block: project name, owner, address, scale, date, drafter name, and contact.
Add notes: applied setback values, reference documents, and any assumptions made during tracing.
Checklist: Ensure SITE_BOUNDARY closed, SETBACKS drawn, ROW shown, Contours labeled, North arrow present, Titleblock complete.

note.label: Success
note.text: Use Layer States to create a PRINT_STATE showing only printable layers before exporting PDF.
